Sentence: You wake to the smell of wet soil and diesel, the kind of smell that lives in TideLab like a memory.
Your overalls are patchwork and damp at the cuffs; salt has crusted along the soles
of your rubber boots.
The merino scarf at your collar is damp where you fell asleep over tide maps, and when you move you feel the stiffness of a night spent hunched over a table full
of sketches.
On the bench, your leather-bound field notebook lies open.
Watercolor tide lines blur at the edges where salt met pigment; tiny, desperate handwriting annotates each smear—dates, heights, the names of places you can't stop visiting in
an absent front door, a shifted pier, a house whose porch now angles toward the bay.
You run a thumb over a small painting of the boardwalk undercut—inked notations you can follow in your sleep.
The name of your lost friend is written in the margin, a vertical stroke that refuses to
the precise, patient ache that shaped the rest of your life.
The shed door creaks; Luka Maren steps in, drone controller cradled like a guilty phone.
He moves as if sound might shatter something fragile—hair tied back, a shy grin that doesn't reach the tired lines beside his eyes.
Morning.
Brought coffee—well, reheated—and the drone's back online.
The gimbal's sticky but—
He holds up the scuffed controller like an offering.
You study his face.
He looks like someone who has been holding his breath and forgot to exhale.
You shouldn't have.
But thanks.' Your voice is blunt, as always—practicality worn like armor.
'Check the battery cycles.
And the firmware; I want a clean log before we fly over the northern planks.
Right.
Numbers.
We'll get them.
Luka Maren's smile thins in a way you read as apology and as hope both.
Yeah.
I ran the diagnostics.
I'll patch the logs.
Also—
He hesitates, thumb worrying a sticker on the controller.
'There's a job inquiry I've been turning down.
You stop him with a look that isn't unkind, just focused.
Later.
Today is panel prep.
The hall wants numbers, not hypotheticals.
We need the boardwalk undercuts quantified, the probability ranges laid out.
No spin.
Right.
Numbers.
We'll get them.
• Trace the tide-line again
You uncurl the page and trace the watercolor tide-line with a fingertip, committing the curve to muscle memory.
The ink is tacky under the pad of your thumb.
• Close the notebook and stand
You close the notebook with a muted thud, leather sighing.
Standing makes the room tilt a fraction—enough to remind you of what riding out a storm feels like.
You breathe the lab's air—oil, salt, a faint sweetness of seedling soil from the greenhouse extension—and for a moment the world contracts to tools and lists and the steady rhythm of work.
Luka Maren moves to a bench and begins to tinker at the drone.
His hands are careful; he treats metal like muscle.
You breathe the lab's air—oil, salt, a faint sweetness of seedling soil from the
greenhouse extension—and for a moment the world contracts to tools and lists and the steady rhythm of work.
The shed door opens again.
Mateo steps in carrying a crate; his hands are sun-leathered and trembling as though the sea itself runs in his blood and remembers roughness.
The crate is full of brittle kelp samples, paper-thin and fragile.
A briny tang follows him in.
Found more along the reef strip.
Didn't look right.
He sets the crate down with a soft curse.
'Whole beds looked… glassy.
Like someone froze them mid-sway.'
You pull on gloves, fingers moving on reflex.
You lift a frond and the kelp flakes, fine salt shedding like old paint.
How many beds?
Enough to worry.
Could be the bloom cycle.
Could be the runoff from the new tanks up the channel.
I don't know.' He rubs his thumb along a callus.
'I thought of you.
Good.
We sample everything.
pH, nitrates, temp profiles.
Don't let it sit in the crate more than an hour.
Bring me the logs from last month and the new bait permits.
Mateo nods, jaw working.
He looks smaller under the lab lights, and the crate seems to take more of his weight than it should.
Abuela Rosa arrives as the first gray light thickens.
She moves with the practiced economy of someone who has walked this shore every season of her life; her shawl is bright against the neutral palette, a
small bloom of color.
She sets a faded map on the bench—hand-drawn markers, ink faded, a coastline remembered before it was on any official chart.
This marker.
My cousin called it Las Olas.
She said when the fifth post falls, the sand changes like a fever.
It is now.
You kneel to look at her map.
Her handwriting curves like tide lines.
People marked spots by where they planted the roses.
We are stories and oaths and rope knots between us.
They come offering concrete and charts; we offer where the ground sings.
Neither is enough alone.
We need both.
And we need to be listened to.
Abuela Rosa studies you, eyes bright as glass.
Listen is earned.
Keep your ledger, child.
But do not let it be the only voice.
wood eaten away, pilings exposed, the shadow of loss lengthening.
You step onto the boardwalk with a notebook in one hand, a rangefinder in the other.
Mud squeezes at the edges of old nails; algae makes the planks shine in absent light.
You mark undercuts with
a practiced hand, the notation small and efficient.
People pass—faces weighed with different brands of worry.
The collective breath of the town moves in small waves, and you are the measuring instrument that tries to make
sense of the pull.
Back at TideLab, as you catalog the boardwalk measurements, Tamsin calls.
Her voice is even, practiced.
Amaya.
I need TideLab's raw logs.
Council is asking for all available data before the briefing.
Voss will submit their render today; we need your measurements to juxtapose.
You feel the ledger under your palm like a small, heavy thing.
You'll have everything I can compile.
Full transparency, but—
You deliberate, choosing words.
'—we need to preserve the integrity of data.
If you pass raw files to Voss, I want to be in the room when they interpret them.'
That's reasonable.
I can't promise Voss won't present their own analysis, but I'll ensure TideLab is credited.
We need numbers that stand up.
There will be reporters.
Reporters are noise.
Make sure the panel gets the variance ranges, not just a best-case curve.
We can't afford a sugar-coated promise.
Understood.
Send the files by noon?
By noon.
• Upload the full dataset now
You open the laptop and begin the upload.
Your hand hesitates over the trackpad, then you press 'send'—an act that feels like letting the tide pull a message out to sea.
• Ask Tamsin for a mediated handoff
You ask Tamsin to set a joint review session before anything leaves TideLab.
Her reply is thin but obliging; it buys you a few hours to scrub and annotate the data you know will be misused.
You sit in the low light of TideLab, watching the progress bars and the slow mechanical breathing of the place.
The sun moves higher but feels as if it's climbing through molasses; everything is slightly delayed, as if the world is holding its breath.
You sit in the low light of TideLab, watching the progress bars and the slow mechanical breathing of the place.
The sun moves higher but feels as if it's climbing through molasses; everything is slightly delayed, as if the world is holding its breath.
Then the holo projection blooms at the edge of the room—a glossy render that seems too clean for this salt-ruined town.
Corinne Voss Good morning, TideLab.
I'm Corinne Voss.
Voss Marine proposes a comprehensive seawall and raised quay system that would secure port infrastructure and regional logistics for decades.
We understand the pain of change.
Our plan is a responsible compromise.
Corinne, this is Amaya Reyes of TideLab.
She'll be presenting community data this afternoon.
Ah—Amaya.
The town's own scientist.
Your work is, well, admirable.
We hope to demonstrate how engineering can spare the larger community from catastrophic loss.
You look at the render.
The seawall is beautiful in its geometry—clean lines, efficient grades—but what it does not show are the houses behind those lines, the fishing nets, the places with names.
The proposal mentions
relocations almost as an afterthought; the render frames it as an editorial adjustment, a necessary pruning.
Your render doesn't show the informal docks, the low-income blocks along South Pier, or the community gardens in the marshland.
How many homes would need to be relocated under your proposal?
We anticipate a small number in the immediate footprint—measured relocations—balanced by long-term security.
Displacement is difficult; it is regrettable in the short term, but the alternative is far worse.
With our model, we protect critical infrastructure that underpins the entire bay's economy.
A low, resigned fury stirs in you, tightly coiled and controlled.
Measured relocations for ports and 'critical infrastructure' is different language for moving people you decided were expendable.
Who decides which neighborhoods are critical?
Urban planners and risk analysts—professionals with the necessary scope to weigh individual hardship against collective survival.
These are hard choices.
Abuela Rosa watches the floating image with a quiet gravity.
Hard choices are not always wise.
There are other rhythms.
You do not listen to the shoreline in models.
We respect local knowledge.
We also respect the need to act at scale.
Engineering can be decisive.
We would like to meet with community representatives—TideLab included—to refine the model.
the friend whose porch was taken in a single night, whose name is the vertical stroke in your notebook.
The guilt loops into a promise—an unspoken covenant to do better, to prevent the last.
We will attend.
But our attendance does not imply agreement.
Corinne Voss's smile does not falter.
The holo shimmers and then dematerializes, leaving a little chill in the air, like the echo of a door closing.
You sit for a long moment with your hands folded around the field notebook.
The tide maps stare back like patient witnesses.
You rehearse evidence aloud—probability ranges, mapped undercuts, socioeconomic overlays—phrases that feel like armor against a smooth render and a practiced smile.
Mateo and his boat, Abuela Rosa and the oral histories she holds, the volunteers who patch nets and plug holes with their own hands.
You think of how easily a glossy plan reduces those names to coordinates.
• Call Mateo and ask him to speak at the hall
You pick up your phone and call Mateo.
His voice is small, then steady as you explain the meeting.
He agrees to come and bring a few neighbors.
It feels like a small, necessary coalition.
• Draft talking points for Abuela Rosa to give the historical markers context
You set the pen to the page and write talking points—clear, short, anchored to place names and stories.
Abuela Rosa will read them like prayers.
• Rehearse the technical appendix with Luka
You turn to Luka and outline how the appendix should sit beside Voss's render—confidence in numbers that might steady a wavering vote.
He nods, fingers already sketching a data figure on a scrap of paper.
practical, unyielding.
The day ahead is a
low-lit corridor of meetings and small confrontations; none of them will be loud, but all will be decisive.
practical, unyielding.
The day ahead is a
low-lit corridor of meetings and small confrontations; none of them will be loud, but all will be decisive.
You tuck the notebook back into your bag, the leather soft and salt-stained.
Outside, the sky gathers iron-gray clouds.
The boardwalk will fill with people; the hall will fill with voices.
You can hear the rain
beginning somewhere beyond the windows—the prelude to something larger, or perhaps simply weather.
Either way, you stand and pull the scarf tighter around your neck.
You are blunt.
You are protective.
You are loyal.
You rehearse the names of who you cannot afford to lose like an inventory, and you step out of the shed.
the ledger in your hand and the ledger of the town are not the same thing, but they must now be read together.
You close the door behind you and walk into the narrow, wet light toward the meeting that will shape Marisol Bay's next beat.
You step into the hall with your notebook under your arm and the salt smell stuck to your collar.
The projector throws a rectangle of pale warmth onto a sheet; TideLab’s tide maps bloom there in
responsibility folded into a kind of tired armor.
The room is fuller than you expected.
Mateo stands by the back bench, shoulders broad beneath his fisherman sweater, jaw working behind a face that never wears more than what the day carved into it.
Abuela
Rosa sits close to the front, her shawl bright against the plain wood, tapping the knit rhythm that keeps her listening.
Tamsin is near the door, tablet clutched like a lifeline — neutral, attentive, worry threaded
into the set of her mouth.
Luka leans against the sideboard, fingers trailing a controller’s edge; he watches the projection but his expression reads small, unreadable shifts.

damp wool, coffee from a thermos someone left near the projector, the metallic, clinical clean of Corinne Voss’s arrival before you even see her — a scent almost manufactured, like citrus-laced antiseptic.
The room tilts.
Conversation thins to a hush.
every line on your maps is an argument, every jar of sediment a witness.
You step forward and place the leather notebook on the table, the spine whispering against the wood.
Thank you for coming.
I know this weather scares some folks into silence, but the maps we’ve been tracking are not opinions — they’re patterns.
The salt wedge is pushing farther into the estuary each season.
Our oyster beds are showing increased mortality in the zones mapped here, and drifting debris is changing where young marsh shoots can settle.
You let the projection linger on the smear of indigo that marks the salt wedge.
Fingers in the room — Tamsin’s tapping, Mateo’s clenched jaw, Abuela’s slow, almost imperceptible nod — answer back.
Amaya, this is thorough.
(She flicks the tablet, bringing up a municipal overlay.) If what TideLab is showing is correct, it has budgetary and zoning implications.
There are vectors here that would require permits to rework tidal channels...
Permits are the paperwork of consequences.
We can’t wait for permits while the salt chokes our nurseries.
Low-tech solutions — marsh planting, oyster socks, distributed berms — are within reach if we mobilize now.
They keep people here.
They keep livelihoods.
Tamsin’s eyes flick to the doorway where Corinne Voss stands, then back to you.
She speaks in that careful planner’s voice, the one that weighs language as if it were a set of keys.
I can push for quicker temporary permits, but I need political cover.
The council will ask for guarantees.
Before you can answer, Corinne Voss steps forward, the holo around her casting an even, cold light.
Her voice is clear, practiced, a blade honed on spreadsheets.
Guarantees come from scale.
Voss Marine is proposing a phased protection system that will secure key infrastructure and the port.
Consolidation is an unfortunate reality, but it's necessary to protect regional economic function and prevent greater loss downstream.
Our model minimizes long-term costs; short-term relocation of vulnerable blocks reduces systemic risk.
Her words fall into the warm projector light and slice it.
The room cools as murmurs rise.
Consolidation — displacing families so a port stays profitable — that is not protection.
It’s a market decision.
You can’t pretend displacement is the only humane option.
Corinne Voss (tilting her head, composed) I don’t pretend anything.
I present outcomes.
The models optimize for lives saved over decades, for ROI that will fund maintenance and future protections across the coast.
Community sentiment is important — but sentimentalism cannot undercut viability at scale.
Mateo’s hand curls into a fist on the bench beside him.
He stands as if pushed by something in his chest.
So whose lives count less?
My dock?
My nets?
You’ll move my family inland and call it efficient.
Corinne Voss (brief, dismissive) We aren’t deciding which two houses are valuable.
We’re choosing the configuration that keeps most people secure.
Abuela Rosa’s shawl makes a soft sound as she stands, her voice rough with years and salt.
We have kept this shoreline longer than your models knew how to count years, señorita.
The sea remembers different things than your calculations.
Do you intend to listen?
I listen to data and to risk.
We are data.
the maps you brought and the glossy, vaulted plans Corinne Voss lays across the air.
You can talk about risks and ROI, but you haven’t walked the marshes at dawn when the air tastes of kelp and salt and the little things — the seedlings, the tubes of forming oyster — matter.
These are living defenses.
Corinne Voss (interlocking her fingers, unruffled) Living defenses have timelines.
We are accountable to immediate threats and to investors who fund maintenance.
If the bay’s infrastructure fails, the cost is national.
We propose a managed consolidation paired with engineered restoration where feasible.
What does 'managed' mean in practice?
Who decides which neighborhoods qualify for protection and which are moved?
Corinne Voss (a small, precise smile) We model for maximal regional resilience.
That’s what the data demands.
You can feel the collective exhale of the hall — a thin, anxious fog.
People shift in their seats.
The projector hums like a second heartbeat.
Luka Maren steps forward then, voice low, measured, an attempt at bridge-building.
Amaya’s maps show weak points that are fixable now.
Small, distributed tech can monitor salinity, seed oysters, seed marsh.
We don’t have to choose between nature and infrastructure if we scale local solutions smartly.
Corinne Voss (with a quick, appraising glance at Luka Maren) Local solutions are valuable as supplements, but they cannot substitute for systems-level protection.
Local devices have maintenance footprints and can be brittle under sustained stress.
Luka Maren (defensive) They’re resilient if we design them to be repairable by the community.
They’re affordable and keep knowledge local.
People can run them, not just corporations.
Corinne Voss (cool) That’s an optimistic narrative.
The reality is complexity and funding.
You have talent — useful talent — but ambition on its own is not policy.
You watch Luka Maren’s fingers close around his multi-tool.
There’s a small tremor in his jaw.
His expression is complex—part concern, part resolve, part a worry that he’s being measured for usefulness and traded.
You don’t
know whether he sees Corinne Voss’s offer as a dangerous compromise or an uneasy opportunity.
The uncertainty stings.
• Show the brittle spots — play the footage Jules took of the nursery die-off
You queue the footage; the room leans in as juvenile oysters fall from their ropes, pale and slack.
Conversations hush.
Tamsin’s hand tightens on her tablet.
• Appeal to faces in the room — call on living witnesses to speak
You invite a woman in the second row to speak; she tells, haltingly, of losing her grandmother’s house to last spring’s surge.
The hall goes quieter; Corinne Voss’s face does not change but her shoulders tighten.
• Highlight the numbers — walk through the salt-wedge graph point by point
You trace the salt-wedge line with your finger; the math is precise, the implications cold.
Some lean forward, others slump in resignation.
The choice ripples but doesn’t decide anything yet.
You take it in, watching who edges closer and who recoils.
Tamsin exhales and taps her tablet again, the light reflecting off the screen like a second set of eyes.
There are three routes here that I can present to the council with community backing.
One is a public mobilization opposing Voss’s plan in full — grassroots marsh restoration and civil resistance.
Second is mediated talks — negotiation that folds community needs into a hybrid development.
Third is a prolonged legal and data-driven campaign aimed at injunctions and regulatory leverage.
You feel the words settle like tidewater.
Each option is a lever.
Each lever moves people.
And if we choose the resistance route, we may face corporate security, political pushback, and immediate hardship in the short term.
If we choose negotiation, we risk ceding land and control.
If we choose legal routes, we expose municipal weaknesses and the town’s private data, and it could take too long.
Corinne Voss (matter-of-fact) Time and resources are finite.
Pick a path and we will respond accordingly.
Pick the one that keeps my nets and my house, not the one that reads best on a ledger.
Pick the one that keeps our stories where the water can hear them.
Luka Maren’s hand finds the notebook you left on the table earlier and opens it, fingers tracing tide-mapped margins as if reading the same prayer you wrote there.
He looks up.
Whatever we pick matters for the tech I can build.
If we choose to confront, I can keep things decentralized and community-owned.
If we talk, I might be pulled into partnerships that compromise that.
If we litigate, we’ll need ironclad chain-of-custody on our data.
I don’t want to lose what makes the work humane.
I don’t either.
And I don’t want to lose you.
His look is a quiet thing — complicated and private.
You avoid casting it in certainty.
You cannot know the shape of his future choices; you can only count the weight of this moment.
• Lock eyes with Luka and give him a small, private nod
You lean in and give Luka a brief nod.
He catches it, a shadow of a smile, then composes himself.
The nod feels like a promise and an appeal.
• Address the hall — ask for whom they want to fight
You sweep your gaze over faces — fishermen, elders, a teenage volunteer — and ask them to voice what they’ll risk.
A woman in the back cries out, and the hall answers with a brittle, shameful honesty.
The room slows.
Rain gathers on the windows, making the world outside feel like a soft, distant thing.
Inside, every sentence feels like a small decision made on the edge of something larger.
You gather your notes.
The projector ticks.
Tamsin’s tablet pings up comparative timelines.
Corinne Voss folds her hands with the calm of someone used to being decisive.
Mateo’s jaw is set.
Abuela Rosa’s fingers stroke her shawl.
Your voice is quieter than you expected when you say it.
We have three choices.
Each risks part of the town, part of ourselves.
I can call the collective to action and organize public opposition — marsh restoration and civil resistance.
I can open mediated talks with Tamsin and Voss to negotiate a hybrid plan.
Or we can build a data-driven legal strategy: share TideLab findings, seek injunctions and regulatory leverage.
not in a storm, but in the choosing.
You wait for the hall to answer, but the decision—your decision—must be deliberate.
It will shape who stands beside you and what Luka Maren’s work will become.
You left the Community Hall with the taste of iron still on your tongue—what you said replaying in the hollows of your skull.
From Chapter 3, Option A.

Plant.
volunteers in borrowed boots wrestle with bundles of mangrove seedlings, clipboards bloom with plotted coordinates, and the battery bank in the corner hums like
a small, patient animal.
The shed smells of wet soil, seaweed, and the faint, sweet chemical tang of salvaged fertilizer.
Your field notebook is open on the workbench, its tide maps annotated in your cramped handwriting;
you set your palm flat on a margin and feel the paper's salt-stiff edge under your skin.
You give orders you did not know you could give.
Your voice is shorter, more precise than it was in the meeting—less rhetorical, more tactical.
Stagger the sponge bundles along the low flats, not in a straight line—curves trap sediment better.
Marta, you and Jules take reef placement two; stagger the oysters in a checker pattern to encourage juvenile habitat.
Mateo, you and I will mark the channels; Luka will fly a grid run and check for subsurface flow before anyone digs.
Got it.
Checker pattern, reef two.
If the tide's high at noon, should we push the grid earlier?
Earlier.
We don't risk washing the seed bundles.
Time the digging to the falling tide—remember what Abuela Rosa taught us about the moonlines.
I'll map the subsurface channels twice.
If there's any unexpected culvert or scar, you'll see it before hands go into the mud.
Good.
Call it out.
If the data and the tide disagree, trust the water.
His laugh is quiet, almost a groan.
He knows you—knows how you make absolutes of cautions—and he knows when to push you to let others act.
Mateo approaches, salt and sun baked into the lines at the corners of his mouth.
He wipes his hands on his sweater and studies the seedlings like a captain poring over a chart.
Never thought I'd be planting instead of pulling nets.
Feels…right in a way, yeah?
It keeps the bay fed and your nets from emptier days.
And keeps you from pacing the entire coastline at night.
I've got a list.
Keep your boots on and your hands busy.
The flurry of preparation leaves no space for long sentiment.
Volunteers tape grafts on sun-faded scarves, tie labels, pass buckets that smell of peat and brine.
The mud is cold and stubborn between fingers; every handful yields a small, apologetic squelch as roots settle.
• Call the volunteers into a quick practical demonstration — keep the briefing short and hands-on
you show the knot, the angle of insertion, the timing with the tide.
Hands follow directions.
The mood snaps into efficient focus and the work hums like a machine.
• Take a breath and give a longer, story-shaped briefing — remind everyone why we're doing this

You let the rain-dampened light settle and tell them about the lost marsh behind Abuela Rosa’s house, about the kid who used to fish with Mateo, about what the bay looks like when the oysters do their work.
Faces soften; hands move with a slower, more careful rhythm.
The work takes on an extra weight of meaning.
'Narrative continues.'
Two meters north of stake C, there's a seam—looks like an old culvert.
Adjust reef placement by three meters.
dig, press the bundle into the mud, tamp, make a shallow dike with spare
mesh.
Every action seems urgent, as if the tide itself is hurried because it knows the town is fighting for time.
Suddenly, a small voice cuts through the rhythm.
A volunteer—Marisol, who ran the bakery before the storms—hurries up the path with her daughter on her hip.
The girl's knee is scraped to rawness, slurry of mud around the wound.
Marisol's face is pinched with apology and worry.
Marisol I—I'm so sorry.
She slipped when we were hauling the mesh.
I didn't see—
a quick clean with salty water, a strip of gauze from the first-aid kit, a hand on the girl's shoulder so she knows it's steady.
you always imagine the worst when small harm touches the people you protect.
You okay?
I'll check the kid's bandage twice today.
Make sure there's no infection.
Marisol, sit—take five.
I'll take over reef placement for you.
Marisol I should've been more careful.
I'm sorry, Amaya.
protocols, bandage checks, reassignment schedules.
The practical response is your refuge.
• Gently scold Marisol for rushing and assign a lighter task to ensure safety
You frame it like a safety correction and give her the quieter job of net-mending at the lab table.
She nods, chastened but relieved, and you feel the guilt subside into the steadier weight of leadership.
• Fold the apology into warmth—share a page of your own small, past mistake to ease her shame
You tell her about the time you misread a tide line and nearly flooded a demo plot years ago.
She laughs through tears; the girl clings to you like a tiny, mud-scented anchor.
The team circles in, human and defiant, and the mood becomes more protective than professional.
'Narrative continues.'
By noon a small delegation from Voss appears on the boardwalk.
They do not march—everything about them is surgical.
A woman in a neutral blazer—steely, efficient—steps forward with a binder of glossy, folded renders and a
jobs, infrastructure, a
grander plan to 'secure' the bay.
The language is clinical benevolence.
Voss Rep We appreciate the community's efforts.
Restoration is important.
Voss can accelerate protections with a comprehensive shoreline program—jobs, coastal defense, and a reinstated harbor economy.
There will be community consultations.
Tamsin stands slightly back, tablet glowing.
Her expression is unreadable—neutral, but the small tightening at her jaw says she's already balancing conflicting pressures.
Consultations don't make up for bulldozers.
Jobs tied to a single corporate plan leave families vulnerable if it fails.
Voss Rep We're offering guarantees.
Relocation is never our first choice.
We want to collaborate.
Scaling up is the only way to protect the broader coast.
fast-tracked.
You
haven't seen the paperwork yet, but the machine behind the smiling face is already running.
They're selling safety like a product.
They're selling certainty.
And someone will pay for it with displacement.
We need to avoid an escalation.
If we block every conversation, we risk control of the narrative—and permits can move while negotiations stall.
I can ask for a delay if you commit to limited talks.
You look at Tamsin.
Her eyes flick to the Voss Rep and back to you—this is the hinge of municipal compromise.
Your instinct is to refuse anything that buys time for Voss.
Your instinct is to
these are not abstract tests.
They are hands in mud and children with bruises.
You feel the old guilt—the one that makes
you vow to carry danger yourself so others do not.
Corinne Voss doesn't speak herself; her presence is in the brochures and the poised representative.
The rep's smile doesn't change, but there's a sharpness in her measuring gaze—she notes who is visibly tired, who flinches, who is immovable.
Voss Rep We also have community outreach programs—training, placement, infrastructure studies.
Think of long-term resilience.
Long-term for whom?
The family that loses their house is not a line item.
The conversation spirals; each word is a small stone thrown into a lake.
Around you, neighbors tighten their coats against a wind that suddenly tastes of rain and salt and corporate varnish.
The drone feed on
an unfamiliar patrol boat circling farther out.
A campus of corporate security would be a bright, moving thing all its own.
• Step forward and demand that the Voss Rep meet one-on-one with Marisol's community circle
You force a public test of their promises.
They exchange polite deflections, and the boardwalk crowds grow louder—voices that want accountability.
For a moment, you see faces in the crowd change from curiosity to allegiance.
• Keep the conversation technical—ask Tamsin for the permit details and escalate through formal channels
request timelines, file forms, push for procedural holds.
It slows the optics, but it also gives Voss time to polish their narrative.
The immediate crowd stays calm, but the undercurrent of anger simmers.
'Narrative continues.'
security advisory—an anonymous tip of 'unpermitted demonstration' filed at the docks.
Tamsin's tablet pings again with the permit
status—fast-tracked, approved for preliminary works in the harbor zone.
The words feel like a slap.
Is that legal?
That fast?
The administration can expedite under emergency provisions.
Corinne's lawyers are efficient.
You feel the room narrow.
Volunteers look to you, waiting for instruction; your refusal to compromise has become a stance.
